speaker: interviewer
text: Step, watch your step.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what I left on.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Roll 4 with Shari Braun. Roll four with Shari Braun. [BACKROUND NOISE] [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] You should tell her to take the medicine a little bit, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Shari, you wanted to tell us about this poem.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell the poem, but again, may I mention something? You were asking about the message. I, one more thing I want to mention, but I-- I learned from my-- my wonderful husband. He said, it's easier to love than hate. I can say the same thing. He's just wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now your sister wrote this poem?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister wrote a poem. We were together, and she felt she could not survive without me. And I-- I did steal a few times. Not from the other prisoners, but I stole from the German. And I give her hope, even though inside I was scared. But somebody had to be stronger, just to show somebody stronger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when did she write this poem? Was it after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. Yes, because in the war, you cannot. You don't have pen. You don't have paper. But you're still fresh with the memory. You know. If I can read it, I tell the truth. And I'm so sorry about my voice. I'm-- I'm so hoarse. That's not my voice. 'We will not forget. It was a rainstorm, a windy autumn night. Thunder shook the earth. Lightning lit the sky. Far away from home, in a concentration camp, my sister and I held each other's hand. Covered up in rags, without shoes or hat, standing motionless by a railroad track. Freezing and shivering, tears filling our eyes, waiting for the train through the whole night. Thousands of prisoners stood in line with us, crying silent-- silently and praying to God. The guard used their clubs and rifle butts. The guards used their clubs and rifle butts. Innocent people were murdered in cold blood. Their lifeless bodies fell on the cement-- on the cement. Their vacant eyes seemed to be addressing heaven. Their blood flowed like a red river, and their soul was drifted toward heaven. By mid-morning, the freight train arrived. We were locked in cage like animals. Behind the closed doors, my sister and I embraced each other and began-- begin to cry. Shari was just a child, but much [INAUDIBLE] in age. She encouraged me to be strong and brave. You will see, she told me. Soon we will be free. But we must believe, and do not lose our faith. She had kept me going and took care of me. Jeopardize-- jeopardize--'

speaker: interviewer
text: Jeopardizing.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Jeopardized her life by stealing food for me. With her gentle kiss, she wiped away my tears. Without her loving care-- [CRYING] a pain [SOBBING] I couldn't bear. Then one Sunday day in May of '45, we were liberated with a new lease on life. But we swore to God the world will not forget our 6 million martyrs who were tortured to dead. Dedicated to my beloved sister Shari whose--' [CRYING] excuse me-- [SOBBING] 'whose unselfish love and devotion helped me survive [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the horror of the Nazi Holocaust. With eternal-- with eternal love, [? Piri.' ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Now would you like to share some of your family photos with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my grandfather, Farkas Mendolovits He was about 70 years old. That was taken in Mishkosh, Hungary, maybe '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. And he was praying. And he obviously there prayed, or reading Talmud. And this picture was taken by somebody. And he didn't know this picture was taken, because he never let him to photograph, because he was so religious. But I'm glad somehow we get it from somewhere. This is my mother, Malvin Kalman, and my father, Fulop Mendolovits, whom unfortunately, I never knew, because I was two and a half years old when he died. And my mother raised us. He's a beautiful human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only I can tell you, because you can tell, it's when they were-- when they get married. I have no idea of which year unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know. Would you believe it?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary, that's for sure. But I don't know it was Miskolc, Budapest, I don't know. This is my wonderful mother. [SIGHS] Malvin-- Malvin Kalman Mendolovits. [SOBBING] My sister, Piroska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- long hair, the taller girl. This was taken in Hungary. And my brother Rudy, Rudolf Mendolovits. He changed his name for [? men, ?] and me, the little girl, Shari, or [? Sharikah. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think maybe you were about nine years old, maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So perhaps around 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine or 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around 1939 or 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have no idea what-- what-- where was that taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the my wedding day in Munich, Germany. And I am in the middle. And I had a black dress with a white collar and a white babushka. I didn't have anything else. I didn't have another dress. So I had to marry to a black dress. And my sister is on the right side?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? Piri, ?] Piroska, and-- and other lady, whom-- a Hungarian lady, Jewish lady-- who was in the concentration camp also with us. We were in the same camp. And that was taken '49, end of '49, I think. And they were the interferer. It was an Orthodox wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1949, so you were maybe 19.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our wedding picture, Shony and I. He had a yarmulke, I don't where it is. I cannot see it. It's a Orthodox wedding. And my babushka came off because we posed for the picture. And Shony got this beautiful flower for me. And I don't know how he got it, because he didn't have any money. Maybe played a song for the-- for the photographer on his violin, because we were very poor. But we were very happy to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year again?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '49, end of '49.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949, yes. This was taken in, here's somewhere in Los Angeles. Shony played somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los Angeles, California?

speaker: interviewee
text: Los Angeles, California, some years ago. And after, somebody asked if he can take a picture, and I-- we said yes. And we got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how many years ago. Some years ago. I don't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the late 1980s, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the '80s, definitely the '80s.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 1980s. And this is a picture of who, again? Tell us the names please.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Shony Alex Braun and Shari Braun.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband and-- and wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, of course, this is his famous violin that he's holding in his hands?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Gagliano. Nicolo Gagliano in his hand. This picture is-- this was taken one of my husband concert, after the concert, some years ago. Maybe in, I'm sure it's in the '80s. And left side, it's our daughter, Dinah, Dinah Griffin. And next to-- next to her, my husband, Shony, Shony Alex Braun. And next to him it's me, Shari Braun. And next to me, Robert C. Braun. We call him Bobby, our son. Our son and our daughter with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken here in Los Angeles, California?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Los Angeles, California, at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre. This is Joshua. He's about six months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's on the far left?

speaker: interviewee
text: The far left. And then [? Sierra. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: In the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is middle. And-- and the right, [? Dane. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who do they belong to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To us. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: To our daughter. And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken last year, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, in my daughter's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: California. Woodlands, California. Woodlands, California. This is a piece which my darling husband, wonderful husband Shony, designed it for me, one, well, I think one of my birthday. And he designed it. This is some ruby, some notes, ruby and a diamond in it. And it says my name, [? Sharikah. ?] And he wrote a song entitled [? 'Sharikah.' ?] And he wrote it to me, he dedicate it to me. And he designed it with the note and with the name. So match the note with the name. [? Sharikah. ?] And became a very nice song.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did he give this to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about, I would say about 15 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is a very, very special piece of jewelry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very special. He did quite a few jewelry designs, because he's excellent. He draws excellent. And-- and paint. He's a very good painter. And-- and an excellent violinist and-- and composer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was-- so this was given to you about 1980?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. '80 or maybe before. Because either he design something for me, most-- or, or when I said, look Shony, I have jewelry. Why don't you, then he ask me, every time he ask me, what do you want for your birthday? Or the-- or anniversary. I always said, write a piece, write a song, write some music. And the music is always going to be, is going to be live forever. Especially how he composes. He's-- he's a very beautiful composer.
